stories.title: Centaur Sells Panther National Golf Resort and a Housing Dev for $191M
stories.excerpt: Ohana Real Estate Investors paid $191.2 million for the Panther National golf resort and a luxury housing development in Palm Beach Gardens, Fla., property records show. The asset spans 392 acres within the Avenir master development at 10391 Panther National Boulevard. Panther National includes an 18-hole championship golf course designed by one of the sport’s legends, Jack Nicklaus, and two-time PGA major champion Justin Thomas, as well as a par-3 course, 68 single-family homes, and plans to add another 150 single-family homes. Swiss developer Centaur Holdings sold the property. J.P. Morgan Chase supplied a $115 million acquisition loan to the Austin, Texas-based, hospitality-focused firm. The deal ranks among South Florida’s largest golf course sales in recent years, though many buyers have ultimately redeveloped their courses into residential communities. In 2021, Centaur Holdings purchased the land for $60 million from the master developer, Coral Gables-based Landstar. Two years later, it completed the 18-hole golf course. In 2024, it secured a $160 million debt package from Southern Realty Trust and Sunrise Realty Trust, of which $85 million went towards refinancing an existing note and the remaining $75 million went towards building out 180 homes and a 60,000-square-foot clubhouse. The debt has been repaid, the lender announced. The purchase comes a year after Brookfield Properties sold the nearby, 800-acre PGA National Resort for $425 million. The property includes 360 hotel rooms, a 40,000-square-foot spa, and 99 golf holes. Representatives for Centaur Holdings and Ohana Real Estate Investors did not immediately respond to a request for comment. Julia Echikson can be reached at jechikson@commercialobserver.com.
bullets: Ohana Real Estate paid $191.2M for Panther National golf resort and 218-unit housing dev in Palm Beach Gardens, FL.
Property spans 392 acres in Avenir master dev; includes Jack Nicklaus-designed 18-hole course plus 68 completed and 150 planned single-family homes.
Centaur Holdings sold after 2-year hold; JP Morgan Chase provided $115M acquisition loan to hospitality-focused buyer.
stories.summary: Ohana Real Estate Investors acquired the Panther National golf resort and luxury housing development in Palm Beach Gardens for $191.2 million, encompassing 392 acres, an 18-hole championship course, and plans for 150 additional single-family homes alongside 68 completed units. Centaur Holdings sold the asset after purchasing the land for $60 million in 2021 and completing the golf course in 2023. JP Morgan Chase provided $115 million in acquisition financing. The transaction exemplifies a broader trend of golf-course redevelopment into residential communities in South Florida.
stories.impact: Golf-course-to-residential conversion signals sustained demand for large-scale residential development and master-planned communities in high-barrier South Florida submarkets. Ohana's acquisition and housing pipeline suggest confidence in single-family absorption in Palm Beach Gardens; however, the deal focuses on single-family rather than multifamily, limiting direct impact on apartment developers—though it does demonstrate capital availability and strong buyer appetite for large residential tracts in this market.

stories.title: Endeavor Building Nearly 200K-SF Austin Offices, to Include HQ
stories.excerpt: Endeavor Real Estate Group is building a new 198,000-square-foot office and retail building on the western edge of downtown Austin. And the Austin Business Journal reports that once built, they will call the building their new headquarters. Dubbed 5th & Walsh, the project has been in the works since 2022. Located at 1200 W. 5th St., it will stand five stories atop 1.4 acres. Of the project’s 198,000 total square feet, 21,000 will be ground-floor restaurant and retail space, and the upper four floors will be office space. Endeavor will occupy 25,000 square feet when it makes 5th & Walsh its new headquarters. Endeavor’s current headquarters is at downtown Austin’s 500 W. 5th St. Besides it office needs, Endeavor says 85,000 square feet of office space is already leased by undisclosed tenants. Endeavor representatives Fernando Urrutia, Anne Swift and Colton McCasland will be handling the marketing and leasing of the office space, while the retail spaces will be overseen by Billy Osherow and Evan Deitch. Join the region’s leading multifamily owners, developers, investors, lenders, and brokers at Connect Texas Multifamily on August 13 in Dallas for a focused discussion on the market forces redefining Texas real estate. Be in the room with the decision-makers driving deals and growth across Texas. Register today: www.ConnectTXMF2026.com. The post Endeavor Building Nearly 200K-SF Austin Offices, to Include HQ appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: Endeavor Real Estate Group building 198K-SF office tower at 1200 W. 5th St. in downtown Austin as new HQ.
5-story mixed-use project includes 21K SF retail/restaurant ground floor; 85K SF office already leased to undisclosed tenants; Endeavor occupying 25K SF.
Project underway since 2022; signals developer and office tenant confidence in downtown Austin core despite broader office headwinds.
stories.summary: Endeavor Real Estate Group is developing a 198,000-square-foot mixed-use office and retail building in downtown Austin (5th & Walsh) that will serve as the company's new headquarters, relocating from nearby 500 W. 5th St. The five-story project at 1200 W. 5th St. includes 21,000 square feet of ground-floor restaurant and retail space, with 85,000 square feet of office already leased to undisclosed tenants and 25,000 square feet for Endeavor's own occupancy. The project has been in development since 2022.
stories.impact: A commercial real estate developer anchoring its own headquarters with a new build demonstrates confidence in downtown Austin's office market and reinforces core-adjacent submarkets as desirable for mixed-use density. This suggests sustained demand for live-work-play districts; however, Austin's multifamily market is already overbuilt. The signal matters less for apartment demand and more as a leading indicator of sustained white-collar employment and talent concentration in downtown/core Austin, which could support adjacent residential development in inner-loop and second-ring submarkets if rent growth rationalizes.

stories.title: NYC Landlords Sue Rent Guidelines Board Over Rent Freeze
stories.excerpt: Following the city’s rent freeze decision in June, it was very much expected that angry New York City landlords — now burdened with even more maintenance costs and increased upkeep on rent-stabilized apartments — would take legal action to overturn the vote. And now it’s a reality. A group of five small landlords who own rent-stabilized apartments in the city filed a lawsuit Wednesday in a New York state court against the Rent Guidelines Board (RGB) over its 7-1 vote last month to freeze rents for both one- and two-year leases on rent-stabilized apartments in the city. The landlords — using the LLC Kenilworth Holdings — claim in the suit that the RGB went through a “sham process” before the vote and that Mayor Zohran Mamdani influenced the outcome of the decision. The suit also claims that the mayor appointed RGB members “predisposed to delivering a freeze,” and that the RGB “ignored evidence of soaring landlord costs and manipulated the data to understate the impact of these costs.” Additionally, the landlords wrote in the suit that the RGB disregarded petitioners who showed that “they and similarly situated landlords cannot survive a rent freeze.” It’s the first time the city’s real estate industry has challenged the legal basis for the rent freeze, and the first time it has legally challenged Mamdani since he took office. “Zohran Mamdani promised to deliver a rent freeze during his campaign and then he went to extraordinary lengths as a new mayor to guarantee it,” Randy Mastro, a lawyer for the landlord group, told the Wall Street Journal, which first reported the news. “This is a perversion of the process.” Spokespeople for the mayor’s office and the RGB did not immediately respond to Commercial Observer’s requests for comment. The lawsuit also cites the resignation of Christina Smyth, a landlord representative and a member of the RGB since 2022. Smyth left her seat on the board just hours before the RGB’s rent freeze vote on June 25, and issued a resignation le
bullets: NYC landlords sued Rent Guidelines Board over June 2026 rent freeze for one- and two-year stabilized leases.
Five small landlords claim RGB vote was 'sham process' influenced by Mayor Mamdani; allege board ignored soaring landlord costs and manipulated data.
Lawsuit challenges political feasibility of rent controls despite rising maintenance costs on stabilized portfolio stock.
stories.summary: A group of five small landlords filed suit in New York state court against the Rent Guidelines Board (RGB) following its 7-1 vote in June 2026 to freeze rents on both one- and two-year leases for rent-stabilized apartments. The petitioners claim the RGB conducted a 'sham process' influenced by Mayor Zohran Mamdani, appointed board members 'predisposed to delivering a freeze,' and ignored evidence of soaring landlord costs while manipulating data. The lawsuit represents the expected pushback from the ownership community against regulatory rent constraints.
stories.impact: Rent freezes on stabilized units reduce yield and discourage value-add investment in older stabilized portfolios across Manhattan, Brooklyn, and the Bronx, but create no direct demand signal for new market-rate multifamily development. However, prolonged rent controls and landlord cost pressures may accelerate conversion or demolition of smaller stabilized properties, indirectly tightening supply and supporting new-build market-rate rents in tight submarkets. For acquisition-stage developers, this underscores the structural headwind of rent regulation and favors new construction in high-barrier markets where market-rate units are decontrolled.

stories.title: National Property Planning 1.1M-SF Katy Industrial Park
stories.excerpt: National Property Holdings plans to develop Katy Prairie Business Park, a 1,083,420-square-foot industrial project in Katy, Texas. CBRE’s Faron Wiley, Billy Gold, Charles Herold and Jett Bush have been selected to market the project for lease.   The Class A project will include two buildings: Phase I, totaling 402,480 square feet, and Phase II, totaling 680,940 square feet. The company developed a Domino’s warehouse across the street (photo) A key differentiator for Katy Prairie Business Park is its favorable tax environment, including potential tax abatement opportunities in Waller County and competitive property tax rates that may help reduce long-term operating costs.  Located at 500 and 700 Igloo Road, Katy Prairie Business Park has visibility along the Katy Freeway, one of Houston’s most heavily traveled corridors, offering exceptional exposure and access. Nearby users include major consumer goods companies, large-scale manufacturing operations, solar energy and emerging technology companies. The post National Property Planning 1.1M-SF Katy Industrial Park appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: National Property Holdings developing 1.08M-SF Katy Prairie Business Park on 1,083K SF across two phases in Katy, TX.
Phase I: 402K SF; Phase II: 681K SF; Class A industrial project positioned for tax abatement and competitive Waller County property tax rates.
Located on Igloo Road with visibility to Katy Freeway; nearby tenants include consumer goods, manufacturing, solar, and tech companies.
stories.summary: National Property Holdings is planning Katy Prairie Business Park, a 1.08 million-square-foot Class A industrial development in Katy, Texas, comprising two phases (Phase I: 402,480 SF; Phase II: 680,940 SF) at 500 and 700 Igloo Road. The project leverages favorable tax abatement opportunities in Waller County and competitive property tax rates to reduce operating costs. CBRE is marketing the project. Nearby tenants span consumer goods, manufacturing, solar energy, and technology sectors.
stories.impact: Large industrial absorption in the Katy submarket signals sustained logistics and distribution demand along the Katy Freeway corridor, a key supply-chain node for Greater Houston. This drives inbound job growth in logistics, warehousing, and light manufacturing—sectors that feed renter demand for workforce housing in proximity-friendly multifamily markets. For Katy-area and Southwest Houston apartment developers, this represents a jobs-growth tailwind supporting demand for class-A workforce housing; the tax abatement strategy also signals favorable local incentives that may extend to multifamily development.

stories.title: New York City Investment Sales Exceeding 2025 Pace: Report
stories.excerpt: New York City investment sales dipped 10 percent over the second quarter of 2026, according to Avison Young’s latest property sales report. Under the hood, however, the city’s commercial real estate market is barreling past its 2025 pace. Overall investment sales in New York City rose annually by 60 percent in the first half of 2026 as capital markets and office financing reopened, according to the report, with transaction counts and dollar volumes rising annually across Manhattan, Brooklyn, Queens and the Bronx. The report did not track Staten Island. The geopolitical and economic upheaval that defined early 2026 did not go unfelt, however, as quarterly sales volume declined to $5.42 billion citywide from $5.68 billion. Above the quarterly noise was a market in a welcome recovery cycle. The city is on pace for $22.87 billion in annual sales in 2026, according to the report, approaching New York City’s 10-year average of $23.4 billion. Brandon Polakoff, a principal and head of New York City investment sales at Avison Young, compared the city’s path of recovery since 2023 to the years following the Global Financial Crisis, which culminated in a 2015 peak for New York City investment sales. “Year by year, we’re working in the right direction,” Polakoff said. Manhattan counted 94 sales in the second quarter of 2026, led in volume by Extell Development’s $451 million move on 405 Park Avenue in May. Other defining deals included Sovereign Partners’ $378 million purchase of 575 Fifth Avenue and the $280 million sale of 250 West 57th Street to Namdar Realty Group. Development was Manhattan’s breakout asset class for the second quarter, according to Polakoff, climbing year-over-year from three to 13 sales totaling $707 million. “We found the demand for development to be astronomical in the past six to eight months,” Polakoff said, pointing to the difficulty of acquiring existing multifamily and the comparative feasibility of stringing together 99-unit rentals to avoid
bullets: NYC investment sales down 10% QoQ in Q2 2026 but up 60% YoY; on pace for $22.87B full-year 2026 vs. $23.4B ten-year average.
Capital markets and office financing reopening; transaction counts and dollar volumes rising across Manhattan, Brooklyn, Queens, Bronx.
Market recovering to historical norms despite early-2026 geopolitical and economic headwinds; quarterly sales volume $5.42B, down from $5.68B.
stories.summary: New York City investment sales declined 10 percent sequentially in Q2 2026 but surged 60 percent year-over-year, signaling market recovery as capital markets and office financing reopen. Annual transaction counts and dollar volumes are rising across Manhattan, Brooklyn, Queens, and the Bronx. On pace for $22.87 billion in 2026 sales, approaching the ten-year average of $23.4 billion, despite quarterly volume of $5.42 billion (down from $5.68 billion in prior quarter).
stories.impact: Year-over-year 60% growth in NYC transaction volume indicates capital redeployment into the market and improving financing availability, removing a key friction point for acquisitions and development financing. This suggests expanding fund availability and refinancing windows for multifamily assets; however, the modest downward sequential trend and office-sector headwinds mean apartment-focused investors should focus on value-add opportunities and market-rate density in high-demand submarkets (Williamsburg, Long Island City, Astoria, Forest Hills) where absorption remains strong despite overall Manhattan softness.

stories.title: Woodside Spins Off 100K-SF Glendale Office Campus
stories.excerpt: Woodside Health sold the Arrowhead Executive Center in Glendale for $25 million. Arrowhead Executive Center comprises 101,397 square feet of Class B office and medical space across eight buildings situated on 12 acres of prime infill land. The property was 94% occupied at the time of the sale. The Arrowhead Executive Center is directly across from the Arrowhead Towne Center Eric Wichterman and Chris Toci of Cushman & Wakefield represented Woodside. The property was acquired by 17235 Piedmont LLC. The address can be traced to Baseline Partners.Located at 17235 North 75th Avenue, Arrowhead Executive Center benefits from excellent accessibility to major transportation corridors, including Loop 101 and Bell Road. Proximity to public transit and major highways enhances commuter convenience and supports workforce mobility. The post Woodside Spins Off 100K-SF Glendale Office Campus appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: Woodside Health sold Arrowhead Executive Center (101K SF, eight buildings, 12 acres) in Glendale, AZ for $25 million.
Class B office and medical space 94% occupied; property across from Arrowhead Towne Center near Loop 101 and Bell Road.
Buyer: 17235 Piedmont LLC (address linked to Baseline Partners); represents office asset spin-off or value-realization by Woodside.
stories.summary: Woodside Health divested the Arrowhead Executive Center in Glendale, Arizona—a 101,397-square-foot Class B office and medical property spanning eight buildings on 12 acres—for $25 million. The asset was 94% occupied at sale and benefits from proximity to Loop 101 and Bell Road transit corridors. Cushman & Wakefield's Eric Wichterman and Chris Toci represented Woodside; the buyer, 17235 Piedmont LLC, is linked to Baseline Partners.
stories.impact: Limited relevance to multifamily development. This is a secondary-market office and medical-office property sale in suburban Phoenix; healthcare-focused spin-offs typically signal REIT or portfolio optimization rather than market-level demand shifts. No material impact on apartment supply, demand, or capital deployment in the Glendale or greater Phoenix multifamily market.

stories.title: Podcast: Building an AI-Powered CRE Operation – Boxer Property President Justin Segal
stories.excerpt: Justin Segal is President of Boxer Property, where he oversees operations, leasing, technology, and marketing across a national portfolio of office, retail, and hotel assets. He co-founded Brava Systems, an AI-enabled platform originally built for Boxer’s own operations and now deployed across the commercial real estate industry, and Relay Human Cloud, a global staffing and team augmentation company. Justin is based in Houston. bullets: Podcast feature on Justin Segal, Boxer Property president, discusses AI-powered CRE operations and technology integration.
Segal co-founded Brava Systems (AI platform for CRE) and Relay Human Cloud (staffing augmentation); oversees national office, retail, hotel portfolio.
Highlights operational tech trends in CRE but lacks specific market, capital, or supply/demand implications.
stories.summary: Podcast profile of Justin Segal, president of Boxer Property, discussing AI-enabled commercial real estate operations. Segal co-founded Brava Systems, an AI platform originally built for Boxer's operations and now deployed across the CRE industry, and Relay Human Cloud, a global staffing company. He oversees a national portfolio of office, retail, and hotel assets from Houston.
stories.impact: No material impact on multifamily development. This is a corporate profile of technology and operational innovation in asset management; it does not signal market-level demand, supply, capital, or cost changes relevant to apartment development underwriting.

stories.title: Mamdani Appoints Lina Khan as Board Chair of EDC
stories.excerpt: In a flurry of announcements on Wednesday, New York Mayor Zohran Mamdani has appointed Lina Khan, the former chair of the Federal Trade Commission (FTC), as the new board chair of New York City’s Economic Development Corporation (EDC). Khan, also a progressive antitrust regulator, will lead the EDC’s 27-member board and help guide the city’s major development projects. Khan was the youngest-ever chair of the FTC during the Biden administration, when she led an aggressive shift in antitrust policy and spearheaded lawsuits against major tech conglomerates and targeted large corporate mergers to reduce consumer costs. “Lina Khan has fought exploitation and dishonesty in courtrooms and classrooms across the country, and given us the blueprint for a government that delivers for working people. As chair of the EDC’s board of directors, I know that Lina will not only steer our administration’s economic development efforts toward a new era of opportunity, but ensure justice is the driving principle behind all of our work,” Mamdani said in a statement on Wednesday. In her new role as board chair of the EDC, Khan will succeed Margaret Anadu, who was appointed by then-Mayor Eric Adams in 2022. Khan’s appointment is separate from the appointment of Anthony Shorris, who Mamdani named Wednesday as the new president of the EDC. Shorris will run the EDC’s day-to-day operations as the city’s primary vehicle for development and infrastructure growth, and oversee projects such as five new city-run grocery stores. Meanwhile, Khan will guide the overarching policy of the EDC and approve its projects. “As Tony shepherds the EDC into a new moment of economic innovation, including the implementation of five city-run grocery stores, one in each borough, I know he and Lina will accomplish a great deal together,” Mamdani said in an announcement, according to Crain’s New York Business. “Frankly, this feels like the economic wonk’s version of the 1992 Olympic basketball dream team.”
bullets: NYC Mayor Zohran Mamdani appointed Lina Khan (former FTC chair) as board chair of NYC Economic Development Corporation (EDC).
Khan led aggressive antitrust enforcement under Biden; now oversees EDC's 27-member board and major city development projects.
Signals progressive regulatory posture toward corporate consolidation and consumer-focused development policy in NYC.
stories.summary: New York City Mayor Zohran Mamdani appointed Lina Khan, the youngest-ever chair of the Federal Trade Commission, as board chair of the NYC Economic Development Corporation (EDC). Khan, known for aggressive antitrust enforcement and scrutiny of large corporate mergers, will oversee the EDC's 27-member board and guide the city's major development projects. Her appointment reflects the administration's focus on 'a government that delivers for working people.'
stories.impact: Khan's EDC leadership introduces aggressive antitrust and pro-labor regulatory posture to NYC development policy. This may slow or complicate large corporate relocations, HQ expansions, and mixed-income projects requiring financial partnerships or tax incentives, and could increase labor-cost pressures on multifamily construction. For apartment developers, the shift signals heightened scrutiny of density bonuses, affordable housing offset trades, and large institutional partnerships; expect more labor-heavy development timelines and potential cost inflation in NYC projects. Regulatory friction may suppress speculative multifamily starts while favoring value-add repositioning of existing stock.

stories.title: AI Job Displacement Risk, AI Office Demand Dovetail
stories.excerpt: U.S. markets defined by sectors most exposed to AI-driven job displacement are also seeing the strongest real estate demand from AI companies, according to new research from JLL. Conducted in partnership with MIT’s Sloan School of Management and Center for Real Estate, the research shows that AI is creating deep divergence across markets, industries, and asset classes, separating those with the capacity to adapt from those without. JLL’s Where AI is Changing Jobs and What it Means for Real Estate research finds that even as overall U.S. tech employment declined by 1.5% in early 2026, office leasing demand in the sector continues to rebound. There’s a clear decoupling of AI growth from broader trends in tech and other office-using industries, according to JLL. In San Francisco, for example, nearly 30% of total leasing since 2025 has come from AI companies, while the city carries among the highest exposure to AI-driven job dislocation risks in the U.S. JLL determined that a market’s capacity to adapt, capitalize on new opportunities and redeploy the workforce is more critical to real estate performance than exposure risk alone. “The winning real estate strategies will be those that look beyond the headlines about job losses and focus on a market and industry’s ability to adapt,” said Alexandra Bryant, global CEO, Value & Risk Advisory at JLL. “It’s no longer about whether a market has AI exposure. It’s about whether it has the right mix of talent, infrastructure, and quality real estate to capitalize on the opportunities AI creates.” The post AI Job Displacement Risk, AI Office Demand Dovetail appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: JLL/MIT research: U.S. markets with highest AI job-displacement risk are also seeing strongest AI company office leasing demand.
San Francisco: 30% of office leasing since 2025 from AI firms despite high exposure to AI-driven job dislocation; overall tech employment down 1.5% in early 2026.
Market's capacity to adapt, redeploy workforce, and attract quality talent more critical to RE performance than AI-displacement exposure alone.
stories.summary: JLL research conducted with MIT's Sloan School of Management finds that U.S. markets most exposed to AI-driven job displacement are also witnessing the strongest real estate demand from AI companies. In San Francisco, nearly 30% of office leasing since 2025 has come from AI firms, even as overall tech employment declined 1.5% in early 2026. The research concludes that a market's capacity to adapt, redeploy talent, and provide quality infrastructure is more critical to real estate performance than displacement-risk exposure.
stories.impact: This signals a secular divergence in office markets and indirect headwind for multifamily in AI-displaced sectors (back-office, software development, administrative roles) but potential tailwind in AI hubs. For multifamily developers in San Francisco, Boston, Austin, and Seattle: expect sustained office demand from AI companies supporting high-wage employment and renter demand in core submarkets, but also workforce churn and income volatility in non-AI tech roles. Markets with strong AI talent ecosystems (SF, NYC, Boston) will sustain premium rents; markets relying on displaced tech sectors (secondary Texas, Midwest tech hubs) face multifamily headwinds unless they diversify employer base.

stories.title: Dallas Apartment Builder Inks $78.7M Refi
stories.excerpt: JLL Capital Markets arranged a $78.7 million construction takeout financing for The Flynn at Live Oak, a five-story, 327-unit newly completed luxury multifamily community in Dallas, Texas. JLL represented the borrower, Conor Commercial Real Estate and Globe Corporation, in securing the loan through funds managed by Blue Owl Capital Inc. Located at 4931 Live Oak St. The Flynn at Live Oak features a mix of studios, one- and two-bedroom apartments averaging 832 square feet. Residents enjoy amenities such as a 2,500-square-foot pool with sun shelf, two outdoor courtyards with fire pits and grilling stations, a sky lounge with downtown views, a fitness center with outdoor yoga space, co-working offices and a grab-and-go convenience store. The property also provides 420 parking spaces with electric vehicle charging stations on every garage level. JLL Capital Market’s Debt Advisory team, representing the borrower, was led by Lucas Borges, Lauren Dow, Sam Tarter, Aidan Flanagan and Christian Johnston. Connect Texas Multifamily in Dallas on August 13 brings together the owners, developers, brokers, investors, and lenders shaping the future of Texas multifamily for a day of market intelligence, networking, and actionable insights. Join 300+ decision-makers and be part of the conversations driving the Texas multifamily market. Register today: www.ConnectTXMF2026.com. The post Dallas Apartment Builder Inks $78.7M Refi appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: JLL Capital Markets arranged $78.7M construction takeout for The Flynn at Live Oak, 327-unit luxury multifamily in Dallas, completed.
Five-story class-A community; financing through Blue Owl Capital; borrowers: Conor Commercial RE and Globe Corporation; located at 4931 Live Oak St.
Amenities include pool, sky lounge, co-working, EV-ready parking; indicates strong permanent-financing availability for completed Dallas product.
stories.summary: JLL Capital Markets arranged a $78.7 million construction takeout loan for The Flynn at Live Oak, a newly completed 327-unit luxury multifamily community in Dallas. The five-story class-A property features studios, one- and two-bedroom apartments averaging 832 square feet, with amenities including a 2,500-square-foot pool, sky lounge, fitness center, co-working spaces, and EV-ready parking. Financing was provided by Blue Owl Capital; borrowers are Conor Commercial Real Estate and Globe Corporation.
stories.impact: Strong takeout financing for completed Dallas multifamily signals healthy permanent-loan availability and investor appetite for class-A product, supporting development feasibility and exit confidence. The $78.7M loan on a 327-unit property (~$241K per unit) suggests healthy loan-to-value and risk pricing for market-rate Dallas apartments. This indicates stable capital markets and refinancing windows for Dallas developers; reinforces Dallas as a liquid, core-plus market for multifamily capital. Developers underwriting new starts in Dallas inner-loop (Oak Lawn, East Dallas, downtown submarkets) should expect similar financing availability for stabilized assets.

stories.title: NYC’s top construction permits: Week ending July 17, 2026
stories.excerpt: 🏆 New Building: The largest new building application filed last week was on the border of Crown Heights and Prospect-Lefferts Gardens. Rafael Rabinowitz of HSD Construction plans to build a 112,279-square-foot, eight-story development at 103 Empire Boulevard. The mixed-use project is slated to have 50 apartments. Kao Hwa Lee is the architect. Job Filing Number: B01408304-I1 📊 New Building: Borough Developers filed plans to construct an 82,731-square-foot mixed-use project at 45-51 and 45-49 Vernon Boulevard in Queens. The building will stand 10 stories tall and have 86 apartments. Kao Hwa Lee is the architect. Job Filing Numbers: Q01425664-I1, Q01425663-I1 📊 […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: NYC top permits week-ending July 17: 112K-SF mixed-use (50 units) Crown Heights; 83K-SF mixed-use (86 units) Queens; 129K-SF two-tower (198 units) Bronx.
Largest alteration: Brookfield Properties 192K-SF work at 250 Vestry Street (Financial District); mixed-use conversions active in outer boroughs.
Steady permit flow indicates continued multifamily pipeline activity across Brooklyn, Queens, Bronx despite regulatory pressure and rent controls.
stories.summary: NYC's top construction permits issued in week ending July 17, 2026 include: Crown Heights mixed-use (112K SF, 50 units) filed by HSD Construction; Vernon Boulevard, Queens mixed-use (83K SF, 86 units) by Borough Developers; and Tremont, Bronx two-tower residential (129K SF, 198 units) by VL Construction Group. Major alterations include Brookfield Properties' 192K SF renovation at 250 Vestry Street (Financial District). Activity reflects continued outer-borough residential development and downtown office repositioning.
stories.impact: Sustained permit volume across Brooklyn, Queens, and Bronx (234 units in three residential projects) indicates pipeline resilience despite rent-stabilization headwinds and regulatory friction in NYC. Outer-borough concentration (Queens, Bronx) and modest unit counts (50–100 per project) suggest risk-averse development strategy and fragmented ownership. For multifamily investors, this signals continued supply adds in high-density, transit-rich submarkets but at slower pace than 2021–2023; expect steady absorption pressure in Astoria, Williamsburg, and outer-borough core nodes, supporting stable rents for class-A product. Larger developers should focus on air-rights, conversions, and value-add repositioning rather than ground-up spec.

stories.title: Related Urban targets aging Coconut Grove housing for two-tower project
stories.excerpt: Related Urban Development Group wants to replace the majority of an aging public housing complex in Coconut Grove with high-rise apartments that contain some affordable housing under Florida’s Live Local Act. The Miami-based affordable housing subsidiary of Related Group filed plans for Magnus Grove and Stirrup Plaza, a two-tower multifamily project with some retail on a 6.1-acre Miami-Dade County-owned site at 3150 and 3160 Mundy Street and 3181 Douglas Road, the South Florida Business Journal reported.  The proposal seeks to demolish a 100-unit apartment building completed in 1975 and a 65-unit building completed in 1982, while preserving a 68-unit apartment […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Related Urban Development proposing Magnus Grove and Stirrup Plaza, two-tower mixed-income project with 750 units replacing aging public housing in Coconut Grove.
Demolish 165-unit buildings (1975, 1982), preserve 68-unit building; replace with 22-story (348 units) and 25-story (402 units) towers; 40%+ at below-market rates under Live Local Act.
6.1-acre Miami-Dade County site near Douglas Road Metrorail Station; eligible for Rapid Transit Zone bypass of local zoning; 7.3K SF retail, 796-space garage.
stories.summary: Related Urban Development Group filed plans for Magnus Grove and Stirrup Plaza, a two-tower multifamily project on a 6.1-acre Miami-Dade County-owned site in Coconut Grove. The development will demolish two aging public housing buildings (165 units) and preserve one (68 units), replacing the demolition footprint with two towers containing 750 total apartments (348 in 22-story Magnus Grove; 402 in 25-story Stirrup Plaza). At least 40% of units will be offered at below-market rates under Florida's Live Local Act, enabling the project to bypass local zoning restrictions on height and density. The site is within Miami-Dade's Rapid Transit Zone near Douglas Road Metrorail Station.
stories.impact: Live Local Act utilization in high-opportunity, transit-rich submarket (Coconut Grove, near Metrorail) demonstrates Florida's regulatory enablement for density and mixed-income housing. Related Urban's acquisition of aging public-housing land signals sustained policy appetite for mixed-income redevelopment and de facto elimination of local zoning friction via RTZ and Live Local Act. For Miami-Dade developers: expect continued public-land-redevelopment opportunities in urban core and transit nodes; Live Local Act creates entitlement certainty and mixed-income arbitrage (40% affordable, 60% market-rate pricing). This accelerates Coconut Grove and similar inner-Miami submarkets' gentrification and supports value-add and new-construction financing for transit-proximate sites. Monitor similar opportunities in other RTZ corridors (Wynwood, Allapattah, Little Havana).

stories.title: BlackRock, MGX buy Plano-based Aligned Data Centers in $40B deal
stories.excerpt: A joint venture that includes BlackRock’s Global Infrastructure Partners and Abu Dhabi-based MGX is acquiring Plano-based Aligned Data Centers in a $40 billion deal. The group is also planning to invest $5 billion in the firm it bought from Macquarie Asset Management. The capital injection is primarily to help Aligned expand capacity, according to Bloomberg. Aligned, who owns and operates data centers across the Americas, including in Dallas, Atlanta, Chile, Brazil and Mexico, has more than 6.4 GW in operational and planned output capacity, according to the outlet. Acquiring Aligned was one of BlackRock’s largest infrastructure investments, and was called […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: BlackRock Global Infrastructure Partners and MGX (Abu Dhabi sovereign wealth) acquiring Aligned Data Centers (Plano, TX) for $40B; $5B additional capital injection.
Aligned operates 50 campuses, 78 data centers (operational + planned) with 6.4 GW capacity across Americas (Dallas, Atlanta, Chile, Brazil, Mexico).
Transaction reflects massive infrastructure capital redeployment toward AI and compute demand; among BlackRock's largest infrastructure bets.
stories.summary: A joint venture led by BlackRock's Global Infrastructure Partners and Abu Dhabi-based MGX is acquiring Plano-based Aligned Data Centers for $40 billion and committing an additional $5 billion in capital to fund expansion. Aligned operates 50 data-center campuses and 78 facilities (operational and under construction) with 6.4 gigawatts of capacity across the Americas, including major nodes in Dallas and Atlanta. The acquisition represents one of BlackRock's largest infrastructure investments and signals massive capital mobilization toward AI and generative-compute infrastructure.
stories.impact: Aligned's $40B acquisition and $5B capital commitment signal record-level capital flows into compute-infrastructure serving AI and large-language models, directly competing for land, power, and labor with multifamily development in Dallas, Austin, Houston, and Atlanta. Data-center density and power-grid constraints in North Texas and Georgia may inflate land costs, labor wages, and power costs for multifamily developers in these markets. Conversely, data-center-adjacent submarkets (north Dallas corporate corridor, Plano, Addison, Atlanta tech corridors) may see indirect job and wage growth supporting premium multifamily demand. Monitor competing land-use pressures and power-grid constraints; expect multifamily to concentrate in transit-proximate, dense urban cores where land commands residential premiums over industrial/compute uses.

stories.title: London’s housing crisis collides with planning system frozen in time
stories.excerpt: London’s chronic housing shortage may have no end in sight. The United Kingdom capital is falling dramatically short of its homebuilding goals as a decades-old planning system, rising construction costs and political resistance stall development, according to the Wall Street Journal. The city targets 88,000 homes annually but started construction on just 4,170 units in the 2024-25 fiscal year, even as its population grew by roughly 100,000 people. London would need another 1.1 million homes simply to match the Western European average for housing per capita, according to the Centre for Policy Studies. The shortfall is pushing first-time home prices […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: London housing construction collapsed to 4,170 units started in 2024-25 fiscal year vs. 88K annual target; population grew 100K despite shortage.
First-time home prices at £672K (~$840K USD), ~10x median salary; renters spend 42% income on housing; need 1.1M homes to match Western European density.
Postwar planning system imposes subjective approval per borough; Shoreditch Works project rejected after four years and 9K pages of planning docs.
stories.summary: London's housing construction severely underperformed targets, with only 4,170 units started in 2024-25 despite a goal of 88,000 annually, even as the city's population grew by roughly 100,000. First-time home prices have reached roughly £530,000 ($672,000 USD), approximately 10 times the median annual salary. Renters spend 42 percent of income on housing. London would need another 1.1 million homes to match Western European housing per-capita averages. The primary obstacle is the city's outdated planning system, where projects are evaluated individually by local boroughs under subjective criteria, leading to years-long reviews and political opposition.
stories.impact: No direct impact on U.S. multifamily development, but relevant as a cautionary case study on regulatory scarcity and zoning friction. London's collapse in housing starts despite massive population inflow and affordability crisis parallels U.S. supply constraints in high-barrier coastal and tech-hub markets. This underscores the long-term risk of subjective permitting, NIMBY opposition, and regulatory fragmentation—lessons for U.S. developers operating in NYC, SF, LA, and Boston. London's case suggests that entitlement risk and timeline extension inflate costs and reduce feasibility for marginal projects; focus on markets with clearer zoning, faster approval timelines, and pro-housing policy (Texas, Arizona, Florida, Sunbelt secondary markets) where regulatory certainty supports development economics.

stories.title: Witkoff, Monroe Capital score $303M loan for huge Miami Worldcenter project
stories.excerpt: Witkoff and Monroe Capital scored a $302.6 million construction loan for a massive residential complex in Miami Worldcenter. Construction is set to begin on the first phase of 700 North Miami Avenue after Witkoff, in partnership with Monroe Capital, secured financing from Athene Annuity and Life Company and J.P. Morgan Chase, Yimby reported.  The transaction refinanced about $57.3 million in existing debt while providing $245.3 in construction financing, the outlet said. New York-based Lotus Capital Partners arranged the financing. Witkoff, led by Alex Witkoff, and Monroe Capital, headed by Theodore Koenig, first proposed the 3.4 million-square-foot mixed-use project in 2022, The […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Witkoff and Monroe Capital secured $302.6M construction loan for 700 North Miami Avenue, Phase 1 of 3.4M-SF mixed-use Miami Worldcenter project.
Phase 1: 894 residential units, 17K SF retail, nine-story parking garage; total project 2,346 units across four towers (50–60 stories); construction cost $270M.
Financing via Athene and JPMorgan; Witkoff/Monroe acquired 5-acre site for $94M in 2021; master building permit under review; site preparation approved.
stories.summary: Witkoff and Monroe Capital secured a $302.6 million construction loan from Athene Annuity and Life Company and JPMorgan Chase for 700 North Miami Avenue, Phase 1 of a 3.4 million-square-foot mixed-use development in Miami Worldcenter. Phase 1 includes 894 residential units, 17,300 square feet of retail, and a nine-story parking garage. The full project will comprise four towers containing 2,346 total residential units, approximately 30,500 square feet of commercial space, and 2,078 parking spaces. Witkoff and Monroe acquired the 5-acre site (former Miami Arena) for $94 million in 2021; construction costs are estimated at $270 million.
stories.impact: Large-scale multifamily financing availability in downtown Miami (Worldcenter) signals capital market confidence in dense, mixed-use urban-core development and Miami's sustained renter demand and investor appeal. The $302.6M construction loan on Phase 1 (894 units) suggests healthy loan-to-cost (~$338K per unit) and strong lender appetite for Miami's core-adjacent residential pipeline. This reinforces Miami's position as a liquid, capital-rich market for major multifamily development; expect continued institutional investor activity and construction financing availability in downtown, Brickell, and adjacent submarkets. For Miami-area developers, financing windows remain open for class-A core and core-plus projects; focus on entitlement certainty and high-barrier sites where scarcity commands rent premiums.

stories.title: Texas construction report: Eli Lilly tops permit list with $378M in filings for ‘Project Houston’
stories.excerpt: New Construction Eli Lilly is gearing up to spend $378 million on a facility to manufacture ingredients for its new GLP-1 pill in Houston. The Indianapolis-based pharmaceutical company filed permits for five buildings under the Project Houston banner, which is set for 12550 Timber Forest Drive. Estimated completion dates for the first five buildings are pegged from summer to winter 2027. The facility is expected to be fully completed by 2030. The total size of the first series of buildings is just under 700,000 square feet. Job filing numbers: TABS2026025404, TABS2026025409, TABS2026025411, TABS2026025406, TABS2026025408 The 344,000-square-foot Franklin Branch Apartments is […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Eli Lilly filing $378M in permits for 'Project Houston,' five-building GLP-1 ingredient-manufacturing facility in Houston; 700K SF; completion 2027–2030.
NRP Group developing Franklin Branch Apartments, 344K-SF, 327-unit multifamily in McKinney, TX; construction start March 2027, completion Nov 2028; $27M cost.
Prologis 517K-SF warehouse in Cypress, TX ($21M); Foxconn $72M office interior renovation in Houston; signals manufacturing and tech job growth across Greater Houston.
stories.summary: Eli Lilly filed $378 million in permits for Project Houston, a GLP-1 ingredient-manufacturing facility comprising five buildings totaling just under 700,000 square feet at 12550 Timber Forest Drive. The project is estimated to reach completion phases between summer 2027 and winter 2027, with full build-out by 2030. Separately, NRP Group is developing Franklin Branch Apartments, a 344,000-square-foot, 327-unit multifamily community in McKinney, Texas, with construction starting March 2027 and completion in November 2028 at a cost of just over $27 million. A Prologis warehouse (517K SF, $21M) is planned for Cypress, and Foxconn is investing $72 million in office renovations in Houston.
stories.impact: Eli Lilly's $378M GLP-1 manufacturing investment in Houston signals major pharmaceutical and life-sciences job inflow, supporting wage growth and employment stability in Greater Houston metro. This follows industry trends of GLP-1 drug-manufacturing capacity expansion and should drive sustained white-collar and manufacturing job demand in northwest Houston and suburban Tech Corridor submarkets. For multifamily developers: expect continued strong renter demand in McKinney, Conroe, The Woodlands, and West Houston; Eli Lilly and similar life-sciences employer expansions support long-term job growth and household formation. NRP Group's Franklin Branch Apartments in McKinney exemplifies this play—workforce housing in proximity to suburban employment nodes. Monitor additional life-sciences and advanced manufacturing announcements in Houston; this sector offers more stable, durable job growth than legacy oil-and-gas, supporting multifamily fundamentals in north and northwest Houston submarkets.
